---
title: "Multivariate exploration of a metabolomics dataset"
author: "Xavi Tarragó Claramunt"
date: "2026-04-06"
output: html_document
---


**1.1 Building the container and selecting samples**

```{r}
# Load libraries
library(SummarizedExperiment)
library(impute)
library(ggplot2)
library(kableExtra)
library(gridExtra)

# Read the expression/abundance matrix
assay_mat   <- read.csv("assay.csv", row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

# Read the sample metadata
sample_meta <- read.csv("sample_metadata.csv",  row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

# Read the metabolite metadata
feat_meta   <- read.csv("feature_metadata.csv", row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

# Convert the assay data frame to a numeric matrix, required for SummarizedExperiment
assay_mat <- as.matrix(assay_mat)

# File contents
knitr::kable(data.frame(
  File = c("assay.csv", "sample_metadata.csv", "feature_metadata.csv"),
  Content = c("Metabolite abundance matrix (120 metabolites × 60 samples)",
              "Clinical information for each sample (group, batch, sex, age, BMI, cholesterol)",
              "Information for each metabolite (name, biochemical class, analytical platform)")))
```

```{r}
# Quick overview of the data

# Number of samples per group (Case / Control)
table(sample_meta$group)

# Descriptive statistics for continuous clinical variables
summary(sample_meta[, c("age", "bmi", "cholesterol")])

# Number of metabolites per biochemical class
table(feat_meta$class)

# Number of metabolites per analytical platform
table(feat_meta$platform)
```

```{r}
# Build the SummarizedExperiment integrating the three components:
#   - assays:   the numeric data matrix (metabolite abundances)
#   - colData:  sample metadata (group, age, sex...)
#   - rowData:  metabolite metadata (name, class, platform)
se <- SummarizedExperiment(
  assays  = list(counts = assay_mat),
  colData = DataFrame(sample_meta),
  rowData = DataFrame(feat_meta)
)

# Print a summary of the object to verify it was built correctly
se
```

```{r}
# Verify the number of samples per group within the SummarizedExperiment
table(colData(se)$group)
```

```{r}
# Set the seed to ensure reproducibility of the random sampling
set.seed(18)

# Get the sample identifiers for each group
cases    <- rownames(colData(se))[colData(se)$group == "Case"]
controls <- rownames(colData(se))[colData(se)$group == "Control"]

# Randomly select 25 samples from each group without replacement (the same sample cannot be selected twice)
# and sort them alphabetically for readability
sel_cases    <- sort(sample(cases,    25, replace = FALSE)) 
sel_controls <- sort(sample(controls, 25, replace = FALSE))

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(Cases = sel_cases, Controls = sel_controls)
)
```

```{r}
# Combine the identifiers from both groups into a single vector
selected_samples <- c(sel_cases, sel_controls)

# Create the subset by filtering the columns of se corresponding to the 50 samples
se50 <- se[, selected_samples]

# Print a summary of the new object to confirm the dimensions (120 metabolites x 50 samples)
se50

# Verify that the group balance is as expected (25 Cases + 25 Controls)
table(colData(se50)$group)
```

**1.2 Data preparation**

```{r}

# Calculate the % of NAs per feature in the assay
pct_na <- rowMeans(is.na(assay(se50))) * 100

# Keep only metabolites with less than 20% NAs as specified in the assignment
se50 <- se50[pct_na <= 20, ]

cat("Metabolites removed:", sum(pct_na > 20), "\n")
cat("Remaining metabolites:",   nrow(se50), "\n")
```

```{r}
# There are still NAs, so we use impute from Bioconductor to handle them
library(impute)

# For each NA value, the algorithm finds the 5 metabolites (rows) with the most similar
# abundance pattern to the one with the missing value, and computes their mean in that
# sample to estimate the missing entry

# Impute remaining NAs with KNN (k=5 neighbours by default)
mat_imp <- impute.knn(assay(se50), k = 5)$data # $data extracts only the imputed matrix from the result

# Replace the original matrix inside the SummarizedExperiment with the imputed one
assay(se50) <- mat_imp

cat("Remaining NAs after imputation:", sum(is.na(assay(se50))), "\n")
```


```{r}
# Plot a boxplot to visualize the distribution of each sample
# and detect samples with distributions very different from the rest
# Apply log2 to stabilize variance and approximate normality
assay(se50) <- log2(assay(se50))

boxplot(assay(se50), las = 2, cex.axis = 0.6,
        main = "Distribution per sample",
        ylab = "log2(Abundance)")

```


```{r}
# Center and scale by metabolite to make them comparable, since metabolites have very different abundance ranges
# The first t(assay(se50)) transposes the matrix so that metabolites are columns for scale()
# The second t(...) transposes back to the original orientation
mat_scaled <- t(scale(t(assay(se50))))

# Store the scaled matrix as a second assay called "scaled"
assay(se50, "scaled") <- mat_scaled
```

**1.3 PCA analysis**

```{r}
# Transpose because prcomp expects samples in rows and metabolites in columns
pca <- prcomp(t(assay(se50, "scaled")), center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

# Variance explained by each component
var_exp <- summary(pca)$importance[2, ]
var_cum <- summary(pca)$importance[3, ]  

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Component = paste0("PC", 1:10),
    Variance_explained = round(unname(var_exp[1:10]) * 100, 1),
    Cumulative_variance = round(unname(var_cum[1:10]) * 100, 1)
  ))
```


```{r}
# Visualize how many components are worth retaining
plot(var_exp[1:10] * 100, type = "b", pch = 19,
     xlab = "Component", ylab = "% variance explained",
     main = "Scree plot")
```


```{r}
# Plot the PCA
library(ggplot2)

# Extract the coordinates of each sample in PC1 and PC2
# pca$x contains the position of each sample in PCA space
scores <- as.data.frame(pca$x[, 1:2])

# Add the group variable to colour the points
scores$group <- colData(se50)$group

# Plot each sample as a point coloured by group
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "PCA - by group") +
  theme_bw()
```


```{r}
# Explore whether other variables explain the observed structure
scores$batch <- colData(se50)$batch
scores$sex   <- colData(se50)$sex

# By batch
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = batch)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "PCA – by batch") +
  theme_bw()

# By sex
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "PCA – by sex") +
  theme_bw()

# By age (continuous variable)
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = colData(se50)$age)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red", name = "Age") +
  labs(title = "PCA – by age") +
  theme_bw()

# By BMI (continuous variable)
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = colData(se50)$bmi)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red", name = "BMI") +
  labs(title = "PCA – by BMI") +
  theme_bw()

# By cholesterol (continuous variable)
ggplot(scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = colData(se50)$cholesterol)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "blue", high = "red", name = "Cholesterol") +
  labs(title = "PCA – by cholesterol") +
  theme_bw()
```


```{r}
# Extract the loadings for PC1 and PC2
loadings <- as.data.frame(pca$rotation[, 1:2])
loadings$metabolite <- rowData(se50)$metabolite_name

# Top 10 contributors to PC1
top_pc1 <- loadings[order(abs(loadings$PC1), decreasing = TRUE), ][1:10, ]
# Top 10 contributors to PC2
top_pc2 <- loadings[order(abs(loadings$PC2), decreasing = TRUE), ][1:10, ]

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Metabolite_PC1 = top_pc1$metabolite,
    Loading_PC1    = round(top_pc1$PC1, 4),
    Metabolite_PC2 = top_pc2$metabolite,
    Loading_PC2    = round(top_pc2$PC2, 4)
  ))
```

**1.4 Comparison with other methods (MDS)**

```{r}
# Compute the distances between all pairs of samples
# t() because dist() expects samples in rows
dist_mat <- dist(t(assay(se50, "scaled")))

# Apply MDS to project the samples into 2 dimensions (k=2)
# preserving the original distances as closely as possible
mds <- cmdscale(dist_mat, k = 2)

# Create a data frame with the coordinates and group for each sample
mds_df <- data.frame(
  Dim1  = mds[, 1],
  Dim2  = mds[, 2],
  group = colData(se50)$group
)

# Plot the samples in 2D coloured by group
ggplot(mds_df, aes(x = Dim1, y = Dim2, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(title = "MDS", x = "Dimension 1", y = "Dimension 2") +
  theme_bw()
```


```{r}
# Add a column indicating the method to each data frame
scores$method  <- "PCA"
mds_df$method  <- "MDS"

# Rename the columns in scores to match mds_df
# (scores has PC1/PC2 and mds_df has Dim1/Dim2)
colnames(scores)[1:2] <- c("Dim1", "Dim2")

# Combine both data frames by stacking their rows
all_df <- rbind(
  scores[, c("Dim1", "Dim2", "group", "method")],
  mds_df[, c("Dim1", "Dim2", "group", "method")]
)

# Plot both methods in the same figure but in separate panels
# scales="free" because PCA and MDS have different scales and comparing them directly makes no sense
ggplot(all_df, aes(x = Dim1, y = Dim2, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ method, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "PCA vs MDS") +
  theme_bw()
```
